paper_title: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding
citation_sentences: Fig. (1e–1h) are TEM bright-field images showing similar results with EBSD IPF images, in which the microstructures are all γ austenite phase. For 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels which have higher Cr content, Fig. 1i and l are dark-field (DF) TEM images revealing nanosized long range ordering (LRO) domains homogeneously distributed in the γ matrix. The selected area diffraction patterns (SADPs) taken along the [011] γ zone axis of the 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels are shown in Fig. 1m and p, respectively. Very faint superlattice reflections can be seen at the $1 / 2 \left\{ 0 { - } 2 2 \right\} _ { \gamma }$ or/and $1 / 2 \ \{ 2 0 0 \} _ { \gamma }$ positions, and satellite diffraction spots around the

primary γ diffraction spots cannot be found, indicating the formation of LRO domains in the online UFCed 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels. No satellite diffraction spots around γ diffraction spots and high carbon content in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels illustrate the formation of the κ-carbides/LRO domains rather than κ′-carbides [14,39]. For 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels, which have higher Ni content, Fig. 1j and k reveal orthorhombic κ-carbides distributed in the γ matrix. The 2Ni steel has the largest size of κ-carbides with an average size of 10 nm. Fig. 1j and k demonstrate that the ordered structures of 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steel are κ-carbides with cuboidal morphologies [21,40]. The low intensity of the superlattice diffraction indicates that the volume fraction of the κ-carbides is very low. However, the thickness of the sheet is uncertain, so a quantitative estimate of the volume fraction cannot be given from the DF.

Mechanical properties are closely related to the evolution of the microstructure. In this study, TEM characterization of Fe–20Mn-9A1- 1.5C-xNi-yCr lightweight steel austenite is shown in Fig. 1. The formation of κ-carbides in austenite is in accordance with the nucleation and growth mechanism, and the different degrees of the ordered phases and the ordered domains of LRO and κ-carbides lead to different yield strengths [20,53]. Previous studies have shown that the addition of Al can promote the formation of ordered phases [19,20,53]. In this study, the steel under investigation contains not only the elements Fe, Mn, Al, and C but also Ni and $\mathrm { C r } ,$ which are added to influence the formation process of the κ-carbides. As depicted in Fig. 1, these two elements indeed affect the phase transition process of the κ-carbides.

Therefore, the addition of Cr into the Fe–Mn–Al–C matrix plays a suppressing role in the generation of the carbides. And Cr atoms take the place of Al atoms to form (Fe, Mn) CrC [54]. This also increases the formation energy of κ-carbides, as well as the interfacial energies and elastic strain energies between κ-carbides and the austenite matrix, causing an increase in the nucleation energy barrier of κ-carbides. This phenomenon can also be found in 3D APT characterization [14] and first-principles calculations [24]. Factors such as the increase in the nucleation energy barrier are the main reasons for the decrease in the size of κ-carbides in Cr-containing Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels. For 2Ni steels, since Ni has an atomic radius of 1.24◦A, Fe atoms have a radius of 1.27◦A, and Mn atoms have a radius of 1.32◦A. They have equivalent atomic radii, and Ni atoms preferentially displace Fe/Mn atoms to form Ni-rich κ-carbides with AlNi C structure (with a formation energy of − 0.181 eV/atom). The formation energy of Ni-rich κ-carbides with AlNi3C structure is lower than that of ideal κ-carbides. The addition of Ni atoms in κ-carbide reduces the formation energy of κ-carbide, lowers the critical formal energy of κ-carbide, and promotes the formation of κ-carbide. The reduction in the nucleation energy barrier is the main reason for increasing the size of κ-carbide in Ni-containing lightweight steels. The results are consistent with those of the dark field images of Fig. 1i–1l in Section 3.1. Since the nucleation energy of the Fe NiC structure is $> 0 ,$ it is less likely for the $\mathrm { F e _ { 2 } N i C }$ structure of the κ-carbides to form in nature.

citation_sentences: Fig. (1a -1d ) and Fig. (1a -1d ) are EBSD inverse pole figures (IPFs) and EBSD phase maps showing the microstructure of the ${ } ^ { 3 \mathrm { C r } , }$ 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, respectively. Fig. 2 is an X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steel. Both EBSD and XRD analyses reveal that 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels are fully recrystallized, and the microstructures are all composed of a single equiaxed face-centred cubic (FCC) structure γ phase consisting of a high fraction of Σ3 twin boundaries, while no B2 phase with body-centred cubic (BCC) structure was found. Excluding twin boundaries, the average grain sizes of hot rolled 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels are nearly equivalent, being 7.63 μm, 8.95 μm, 8.57 μm and 7.60 μm, respectively. When they are included, the average grain sizes are 3.51 μm, 3.73 μm, 3.72 μm and 2.2 μm.

citation_sentences: Figs. (3a, 3c, 3e and 3g) are atomic resolution HRTEM images along the [011]γ zone axis showing the microstructures of the LRO domains in 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels (Fig. 3a and $_ { g ) }$ and the κ-carbides in 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steel (Fig. 3c and $\mathbf { e } ) ,$ respectively. The inset patterns in Fig. 3a, c, e and g are fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns. To more clearly and directly reveal these LRO domains in the HRTEM imaging, inverse FFT (IFFT) images were obtained using only superlattice reflections, as shown in Fig. 3b, d, 3f and 3h. As shown in Fig. 3b and h, the microstructure of LRO domains is ellipsoidal, and the spatially localized LRO domains have an interconnected morphology, which is often observed in spinodal decomposition. The average sizes of the LRO domains in 3Cr steel and 2Ni5Cr steel measured using IFFT images are approximately 1.75 nm and 2.14 nm, respectively. The average sizes of these LRO domains and κ-carbides determined by measuring more than 200 domains by Image-Pro Plus software are plotted in Fig. 3i. Their average sizes decrease from \~10.69 nm through \~5.46 nm and \~2.14 nm–\~1.75 nm for the 2Ni steel, 2Ni3Cr steel, 2Ni5Cr steel and 3Cr steel.

In Fig. 3, it can be seen that the size of κ-carbide decreases with increasing content of Cr elements. We hypothesize that the addition of Cr will negatively impact the formation of κ-carbide/LRO domains, whereas Ni will promote the generation of κ-carbides. This idea will be demonstrated in Section 4.1 and was also confirmed in the text [24,41].

caption: Fig. 4. Tensile properties of 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steel. (a) Engineering stress–strain curves. (b) True stress–strain curves. (c) Strain hardening curves. (d) TS × TEL versus tensile strength. The experimental data for Fe-Al [42], Fe-Mn [43,44], Fe–Mn–C [45,46,47], Fe–Mn–Al–C [14,36,45,46,48] and stainless steels [49,50] are compared with those for the present Ni&Cr-alloyed steels, and the raw data for the tensile test are given in Table 2.

paper_title: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding
citation_sentences: 62.07 GPa⋅%. Compared with 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steel, the yield and tensile strengths of 2Ni steels are 1202 MPa and 1283 MPa, respectively, and those of the 3Cr steels with no added Ni, are even lower, at 969 MPa and 1141 MPa, respectively. Regarding the mechanical properties of the present Ni–Cr-alloyed steel, other single-phase and two-phase lightweight steels, conventional TWIP steels and stainless steels [42–47, 49–51] is shown in Fig. 4d and Table 2. This shows that the superior combination of strength and ductility of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C-xNi-yCr steels distinguishes them from other steels, especially in terms of a significant increase in yield strength for the same GPa⋅% or total elongation. Obviously, for the present Ni and Cr alloy steels with equivalent grain sizes, the increase in strength with the addition of Cr and Ni should be associated with the formation of LRO domains or κ-carbides.

The true stress‒strain curves and work-hardening curves of 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steel are shown in Fig. 4b and c, respectively. There are two types of work-hardening curves between the work-hardening curves of steels. One type includes the black and green curves representing 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steel, respectively, and the other includes the blue and red curves representing 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels, respectively. The hollow dots in Fig. 4c indicate that there are three strain hardening stages (Stages I, II, and III) as the true strain increases, as observed for the four steels. During Stage I, the strain hardening rate of the four steels decreases sharply with increasing strain. During Stage II, the strain hardening rate tends to increase with increasing strain, and Stage II is prolonged compared among the 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels with increasing Cr content. The strain hardening rate of the four steels in Stage III decreases, and the work hardening rate of 2Ni5Cr is higher than that of the 2Ni and 2Ni3Cr steels with increasing Cr content in the process of Stage III. During Stage III, 2Ni3Cr steel has a higher work hardening rate than 2Ni steel. Regarding the Ni comment, comparing the 3Cr and 2Ni3Cr steels, as the Ni content increases, Stage II and Stage III are shortened and lowered. These changes in the strain hardening behavior should be responsible for the significant enhancement of tensile strength and ductility with the addition of Ni and Cr.

According to the experimental results, the evolution of the deformation-induced microstructure with increasing strain for 2Ni and 3Cr steels is schematically shown in Fig. 12. Fig. 12a–c show the case of 3Cr steel. At the initial stage of plastic deformation, the steel shows planar dislocation activity. For dislocations that initially appear slide in a planar feature, the activity of subsequent dislocations tends to be constrained within the planar slip bands (Fig. 6a–c and Fig. 12a), and the strain hardening rate decreases rapidly, followed by an increase with increasing strain (Figs. 6b and 12a), which is mainly attributed to the larger mean free path of the dislocations. A limited number of dislocation annihilation events at grain boundaries are also expected to lead to a decrease in the strain hardening rate. When entering strain-hardening stage II (Fig. 6d–f), plastic deformation is characterized by the appearance and proliferation of HDDA (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b). With a further increase in strain, the microstructural evolution is characterized by mutual annihilation and cross-slip through sliding dislocations (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b) and by refinement of the slip bands (Figs. 6d and 12b). This corresponds to strain-hardening stage III (Fig. 4c), where the strainhardening rate decreases with increasing strain and is dominated by a high density of dislocations (Figs. 6g and 12c) and very fine slip bands (Figs. 6h and 12c). It should also be noted that stacking fault and deformation twins also occur in 3Cr steels (Fig. 7i–l and Fig. 12c), but only at very high strain levels near ultimate failure.

A large number of dimples can be observed in the fibre zone of the four steels. This indicates that they all fracture in a ductile mode.

citation_sentences: To further study substructure evolution at different stages of plastic deformation, TEM specimens were prepared from interrupted test specimens and tensile specimens tested to fracture. Fig. 6 represents the evolutionary characteristics of the substructural deformation of 3Cr steel at different engineering strain stages. The bright field TEM images were taken along the $[ 0 1 1 ] _ { \gamma }$ zone axis of the FCC matrix phase, and the inset shows a selected area electron diffraction pattern. As shown in Fig. 6a–c, when the strain reaches 3%, dislocation slip is the main feature, while the [011](1-11) slip system in the grain has a Schmid factor of 0.52. The dislocation slip has a strong planarity, such as dislocation multipoles localized within {111} slip bands (Fig. 6a), dislocations constrained within planar (1-11) slip bands and restricted by the planar slip bands (Fig. 6b) and the interaction of (1-11) and (11-1) slip bands from different slip systems (Fig. 6c), and has also been observed in other FCC alloys with similar phenomena [52].

With strain accumulating to 10%, dislocation slip is also the main feature, while the slip bands are mainly (1-11) slip bands. It is also illustrated that the space between the slip bands becomes narrower, the number of slip bands increases, and the dislocation density increases, as shown in Fig. 6d. There are also more slip systems operated, and planar slip bands from different slip systems appear as (1-11) and (11-1) slip surfaces (Fig. 6e and f). They interrupt each other. (1-11) and (11-1) slip bands, which carry high-density dislocation arrays (HDDA); HDDA is restricted by the (1-11) and (11-1) planar slip bands (Fig. 6e).

When the strain reaches 54% (LD), the microstructural evolution is shown in Fig. 6g–l. High-density dislocations and very fine slip bands can be observed (Fig. 6g–h), and a dozen nanometre-width deformation twins are seen in the bright-field image (Fig. 6i), the selected electron diffraction pattern (in the inset of Fig. 6i) and TEM high-resolution images (Fig. 6j). Dozens of nanometre-width stacking faults can be observed in the bright-field image (Fig. 6k), the selected electron diffraction pattern (in the inset of Fig. 6k) and the dark-field image (Fig. 6l). Since the deformation twins appear only in the vicinity of the fracture surfaces, their contribution to the overall ductility and strainhardening capacity is expected to be very limited. This implies that 3Cr steels are dominated by HDDA and dislocation slip during deformation, and their strain hardenability is mainly contributed by HDDA densification and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR).

According to the experimental results, the evolution of the deformation-induced microstructure with increasing strain for 2Ni and 3Cr steels is schematically shown in Fig. 12. Fig. 12a–c show the case of 3Cr steel. At the initial stage of plastic deformation, the steel shows planar dislocation activity. For dislocations that initially appear slide in a planar feature, the activity of subsequent dislocations tends to be constrained within the planar slip bands (Fig. 6a–c and Fig. 12a), and the strain hardening rate decreases rapidly, followed by an increase with increasing strain (Figs. 6b and 12a), which is mainly attributed to the larger mean free path of the dislocations. A limited number of dislocation annihilation events at grain boundaries are also expected to lead to a decrease in the strain hardening rate. When entering strain-hardening stage II (Fig. 6d–f), plastic deformation is characterized by the appearance and proliferation of HDDA (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b). With a further increase in strain, the microstructural evolution is characterized by mutual annihilation and cross-slip through sliding dislocations (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b) and by refinement of the slip bands (Figs. 6d and 12b). This corresponds to strain-hardening stage III (Fig. 4c), where the strainhardening rate decreases with increasing strain and is dominated by a high density of dislocations (Figs. 6g and 12c) and very fine slip bands (Figs. 6h and 12c). It should also be noted that stacking fault and deformation twins also occur in 3Cr steels (Fig. 7i–l and Fig. 12c), but only at very high strain levels near ultimate failure.

citation_sentences: According to the experimental results, the evolution of the deformation-induced microstructure with increasing strain for 2Ni and 3Cr steels is schematically shown in Fig. 12. Fig. 12a–c show the case of 3Cr steel. At the initial stage of plastic deformation, the steel shows planar dislocation activity. For dislocations that initially appear slide in a planar feature, the activity of subsequent dislocations tends to be constrained within the planar slip bands (Fig. 6a–c and Fig. 12a), and the strain hardening rate decreases rapidly, followed by an increase with increasing strain (Figs. 6b and 12a), which is mainly attributed to the larger mean free path of the dislocations. A limited number of dislocation annihilation events at grain boundaries are also expected to lead to a decrease in the strain hardening rate. When entering strain-hardening stage II (Fig. 6d–f), plastic deformation is characterized by the appearance and proliferation of HDDA (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b). With a further increase in strain, the microstructural evolution is characterized by mutual annihilation and cross-slip through sliding dislocations (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b) and by refinement of the slip bands (Figs. 6d and 12b). This corresponds to strain-hardening stage III (Fig. 4c), where the strainhardening rate decreases with increasing strain and is dominated by a high density of dislocations (Figs. 6g and 12c) and very fine slip bands (Figs. 6h and 12c). It should also be noted that stacking fault and deformation twins also occur in 3Cr steels (Fig. 7i–l and Fig. 12c), but only at very high strain levels near ultimate failure.

As shown in Fig. 12d–f, the deformation mechanisms of 2Ni steel are mainly mediated by plane slip. In this steel, the slip bands are relatively uniformly distributed (Fig. 7a–b and Fig. 12d), such slip bands multiply with increasing strain, and the space of the slip band decreases (Fig. 7d–f and Fig. 12f). It results that strain hardening rate increase with the strain increasing, and the strain hardening stage is shortened than 3Cr steel. At strain-hardening Stage II, the strain-hardening rate increases with increasing strain. When entering strain-hardening Stage III, it is expected to be due to accelerated dynamic recovery and hard-to-dynamic slip band refinement (Fig. 7i). The dynamic recovery corresponds to dislocations on planar slip bands that are close enough or sufficiently high stresses for dislocation annihilation to occur during cross-slip. A small number of stack faults were found at very high strain levels close to final failure (Fig. 7j–l and Fig. 12e). Overall, Cr alloying of Fe–Mn–Al–C steels promotes the DSBR and HDDA effects of plastic deformation, which can increase elongation.

citation_sentences: For studying phase transformation and thermodynamic properties, formation energy is a crucial parameter as it relates to the stability and equilibrium of the κ-carbides. This study analyzed the effect of Ni and Cr on LRO and κ-carbides in Fe–20Mn-9A1-1.5C steel by first-principle calculations for the Fe–20Mn-9A1-1.5C lightweight steel studied. The possible structures and their energies for the Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C steel were calculated by the Materials Project database (https://legacy.ma terialsproject.org/). The Materials Project database employs density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [38]. Fig. 9 shows the crystal structures of (a) disordered $\gamma ,$ (b) ordered γ, (c) κ-carbide, and (d) AlNi C (one of the Ni-rich κ-carbide). In Fe–20Mn-9A1-1.5C-xNi-yCr lightweight austenitic steels, the Fe, Mn, and Al atoms randomly occupy the vertex and face-centre positions of the face-centred cubic lattice (Fig. 9a). When the Al element occupies the position of the vertex and the Fe and Mn elements occupy the face centre positions of the cubic lattice, the ordered γ-austenite phase is formed (Fig. 9b). When elemental C gradually occupies the body-centred (1/2, 1/2, 1/2) octahedral interstitials, κ-carbides are formed. κ-carbides are perovskite crystal structures, also known as $\operatorname { E 2 } _ { 1 }$ structures [29]. The ideal stoichiometry of κ-carbides is (Fe, Mn) AlC [29]. The results of the first-principle calculations for the possible formation of κ-carbides structure are shown in Table 3.

citation_sentences: Here, we assume that the larger of the two factors $( \sigma _ { \mathrm { o r d e r } }$ and σcoh+σmod) contributes to the precipitation strengthening [64]. Among these, $\sigma _ { \mathrm { c o h } } + \sigma _ { \mathrm { m o d } }$ peaks when shear dislocations approach the interface between the matrix and the precipitate, while σ peaks when half of the precipitate is sheared by the dislocation [62]. We calculate $\sigma _ { \mathrm { o r d e r } }$ and $\sigma _ { \mathrm { c o h } } \mathrm { + } \sigma _ { \mathrm { m o d } }$ using the size and volume fraction of nanodomains and κ-carbides, taking the maximum value as $\sigma _ { \mathrm { p r e p } } $ . We ultimately predict the yield strength using size distribution $\left( r _ { \mathrm { i } } \right)$ and the volume fraction (f ) of κ-carbides of different sizes, comparing it with the experimental results of tensile tests. The calculated $\sigma _ { \mathrm { p r e p } }$ is shown in Fig. 10, where the red dashed line represents the transition radius, at which the dominant strengthening mechanism shifts from $\sigma _ { \mathrm { o r d e r } }$ to $\sigma _ { \mathrm { c o h } } + \sigma _ { \mathrm { m o d } } .$ . For the ${ } ^ { 3 \mathrm { C r } , }$ , 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, the strength contribution of $\sigma _ { \mathrm { c o h } } + \sigma _ { \mathrm { m o d } }$ is dominant. However, in the 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr samples with LRO nanodomains, $\sigma _ { \mathrm { o r d e r } }$ also contributes to $\sigma _ { \mathrm { p r e p } }$ because some of the nanodomains are smaller than the critical size.

paper_title: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding
citation_sentences: Based on Equation (1), we summarize the strengthening effects for the 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr samples. Fig. 11 compares the results with the measured yield strengths, illustrating the contribution to the yield strength of each strengthening mechanism for each sample. There is a correlation between the calculated and experimental values of yield strength and the predicted and measured yield strengths, which are in very good agreement. Solution strengthening is evidently the primary strengthening mechanism for these samples, with the enhancement in yield strength for traditional Fe–Mn–Al–C steels being closely associated with a carbon content as high as 1.5%. Precipitation strengthening also plays a significant role in these samples, with the contribution from Ni and Cr elements synergistically in Fe–Mn–Al–C steel providing an increase in the size of the precipitate phase, leading to a gradual increase in precipitation strengthening as the precipitate size grows.

caption: Fig. 12. Schematic diagram showing deformation microstructure at different strain stages in (a–c) 3Cr steel and (d–e) 2Ni steel.

paper_title: Enhancing strength and ductility in Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C austenitic low-density steels through Ni and Cr alloying synergy adding
citation_sentences: According to the experimental results, the evolution of the deformation-induced microstructure with increasing strain for 2Ni and 3Cr steels is schematically shown in Fig. 12. Fig. 12a–c show the case of 3Cr steel. At the initial stage of plastic deformation, the steel shows planar dislocation activity. For dislocations that initially appear slide in a planar feature, the activity of subsequent dislocations tends to be constrained within the planar slip bands (Fig. 6a–c and Fig. 12a), and the strain hardening rate decreases rapidly, followed by an increase with increasing strain (Figs. 6b and 12a), which is mainly attributed to the larger mean free path of the dislocations. A limited number of dislocation annihilation events at grain boundaries are also expected to lead to a decrease in the strain hardening rate. When entering strain-hardening stage II (Fig. 6d–f), plastic deformation is characterized by the appearance and proliferation of HDDA (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b). With a further increase in strain, the microstructural evolution is characterized by mutual annihilation and cross-slip through sliding dislocations (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b) and by refinement of the slip bands (Figs. 6d and 12b). This corresponds to strain-hardening stage III (Fig. 4c), where the strainhardening rate decreases with increasing strain and is dominated by a high density of dislocations (Figs. 6g and 12c) and very fine slip bands (Figs. 6h and 12c). It should also be noted that stacking fault and deformation twins also occur in 3Cr steels (Fig. 7i–l and Fig. 12c), but only at very high strain levels near ultimate failure.

As shown in Fig. 12d–f, the deformation mechanisms of 2Ni steel are mainly mediated by plane slip. In this steel, the slip bands are relatively uniformly distributed (Fig. 7a–b and Fig. 12d), such slip bands multiply with increasing strain, and the space of the slip band decreases (Fig. 7d–f and Fig. 12f). It results that strain hardening rate increase with the strain increasing, and the strain hardening stage is shortened than 3Cr steel. At strain-hardening Stage II, the strain-hardening rate increases with increasing strain. When entering strain-hardening Stage III, it is expected to be due to accelerated dynamic recovery and hard-to-dynamic slip band refinement (Fig. 7i). The dynamic recovery corresponds to dislocations on planar slip bands that are close enough or sufficiently high stresses for dislocation annihilation to occur during cross-slip. A small number of stack faults were found at very high strain levels close to final failure (Fig. 7j–l and Fig. 12e). Overall, Cr alloying of Fe–Mn–Al–C steels promotes the DSBR and HDDA effects of plastic deformation, which can increase elongation.
